alternateName: From the paper: hotel booking demand datasets

distribution.name: archive.zip
distribution.description: Archive containing all the contents of the Hotel booking demand dataset

distribution.name: hotel_bookings.csv
distribution.description: This data set contains a single file which compares various booking information between two hotels: a city hotel and a resort hotel.

field.name: hotel
field.description: Hotel (H1 = Resort Hotel or H2 = City Hotel)

field.name: is_canceled
field.description: Value indicating if the booking was canceled (1) or not (0)

field.name: lead_time
field.description: Number of days that elapsed between the entering date of the booking into the PMS and the arrival date

field.name: arrival_date_year
field.description: Year of arrival date

field.name: arrival_date_month
field.description: Month of arrival date

field.name: arrival_date_week_number
field.description: Week number of year for arrival date

field.name: arrival_date_day_of_month
field.description: Day of arrival date

field.name: stays_in_weekend_nights
field.description: Number of weekend nights (Saturday or Sunday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel

field.name: stays_in_week_nights
field.description: Number of week nights (Monday to Friday) the guest stayed or booked to stay at the hotel

field.name: adults
field.description: Number of adults

field.name: children
field.description: Number of children

field.name: babies
field.description: Number of babies

field.name: meal
country
field.description: Country of origin. Categories are represented in the ISO 3155–3:2013 format

field.name: market_segment
field.description: Market segment designation. In categories, the term “TA” means “Travel Agents” and “TO” means “Tour Operators”

field.name: distribution_channel
field.description: Booking distribution channel. The term “TA” means “Travel Agents” and “TO” means “Tour Operators”

field.name: is_repeated_guest
field.description: Value indicating if the booking name was from a repeated guest (1) or not (0)

field.name: previous_cancellations
field.description: Number of previous bookings that were cancelled by the customer prior to the current booking

field.name: previous_bookings_not_canceled
field.description: Number of previous bookings not cancelled by the customer prior to the current booking

field.name: reserved_room_type
field.description: Code of room type reserved. Code is presented instead of designation for anonymity reasons.

field.name: assigned_room_type
field.description: Code for the type of room assigned to the booking. Sometimes the assigned room type differs from the reserved room type due to hotel operation reasons (e.g. overbooking) or by customer request. Code is presented instead of designation for anonymity reasons.

field.name: booking_changes
field.description: Number of changes/amendments made to the booking from the moment the booking was entered on the PMS until the moment of check-in or cancellation

field.name: deposit_type
agent
field.description: ID of the travel agency that made the booking

field.name: company
field.description: ID of the company/entity that made the booking or responsible for paying the booking. ID is presented instead of designation for anonymity reasons

field.name: days_in_waiting_list
field.description: Number of days the booking was in the waiting list before it was confirmed to the customer

field.name: customer_type
adr
field.description: Average Daily Rate as defined by dividing the sum of all lodging transactions by the total number of staying nights

field.name: required_car_parking_spaces
field.description: Number of car parking spaces required by the customer

field.name: total_of_special_requests
field.description: Number of special requests made by the customer (e.g. twin bed or high floor)

field.name: reservation_status
reservation_status_date
field.description: Date at which the last status was set. This variable can be used in conjunction with the ReservationStatus to understand when was the booking canceled or when did the customer checked-out of the hotel

recordSet.name: hotel_bookings.csv
recordSet.description: This data set contains a single file which compares various booking information between two hotels: a city hotel and a resort hotel.

keywords: subject > people and society > business > travel
subject > people and society > business > travel > hotels and accommodations
subject > people and society > social science
name: Hotel booking demand
description:
### Context

Have you ever wondered when the best time of year to book a hotel room is? Or the optimal length of stay in order to get the best daily rate? What if you wanted to predict whether or not a hotel was likely to receive a disproportionately high number of special requests?

This hotel booking dataset can help you explore those questions!

### Content

This data set contains booking information for a city hotel and a resort hotel, and includes information such as when the booking was made, length of stay, the number of adults, children, and/or babies, and the number of available parking spaces, among other things.

All personally identifying information has been removed from the data.

### Acknowledgements

The data is originally from the article [**Hotel Booking Demand Datasets**](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2352340918315191), written by Nuno Antonio, Ana Almeida, and Luis Nunes for Data in Brief, Volume 22, February 2019.

The data was downloaded and cleaned by Thomas Mock and Antoine Bichat for [#TidyTuesday during the week of February 11th, 2020](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/master/data/2020/2020-02-11/readme.md). 

### Inspiration

This data set is ideal for anyone looking to practice their exploratory data analysis (EDA) or get started in building predictive models!

If you're looking for inspiration on data visualizations, check out the [#TidyTuesday program](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday), a free, weekly online event that encourages participants to create and share their [code and visualizations for a given data set on Twitter](https://twitter.com/search?q=%23TidyTuesday&src=typed_query).

If you'd like to dive into predictive modeling, [Julia Silge](https://twitter.com/juliasilge) has an [accessible and fantastic walk-through](https://juliasilge.com/blog/hotels-recipes/) which highlights the [`tidymodels`](https://www.tidyverse.org/blog/2018/08/tidymodels-0-0-1/) R package.
